perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is to be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware without regard to the principles of conflicts of law that would cause the laws of any other jurisdiction to be applicable.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is to be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, even if the principles of conflicts of law would cause the laws of any other jurisdiction to be applicable.
perturbation.explanation: This clause mandates Delaware law even when other jurisdictions have stronger connections to the transaction, potentially violating consumer protection laws in those other jurisdictions. By removing the consideration of conflicts of law principles, the contract attempts to bypass laws that might otherwise apply and protect consumers.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Choice of Law and Consumer Protection Statutes
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the principles of consumer protection which dictate that choice of law provisions should not be used to circumvent consumer protection laws of jurisdictions with closer ties to the transaction, as outlined in several consumer protection statutes. This is relevant as the Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act indicates minimum standards across states regarding warranties, and strict application of Delaware law could bypass these if the product is sold and used elsewhere.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 15 / CHAPTER 50 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 15 USC Ch. 50 : CONSUMER PRODUCT WARRANTIES From Title 15—COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 50 —CONSUMER PRODUCT WARRANTIES Sec. 2301. Definitions. 2302. Rules governing contents of warranties. 2303. Designation of written warranties. 2304. Federal minimum standards for warranties. 2305. Full and limited warranting of a consumer product. 2306. Service contracts; rules for full, clear and conspicuous disclosure of terms and conditions; addition to or in lieu of written warranty. 2307. Designation of representatives by warrantor to perform duties under written or implied warranty. 2308. Implied warranties. 2309. Procedures applicable to promulgation of rules by Commission. 2310. Remedies in consumer disputes. 2311. Applicability to other laws. 2312. Effective dates. §2301. Definitions For the purposes of this chapter: (1) The term "consumer product" means any tangible personal property which is distributed in commerce and which is normally used for personal, family, or household purposes (including any such property intended to be attached to or installed in any real property without regard to whether it is so attached or installed). (2) The term "Commission" means the Federal Trade Commission. (3) The term "consumer" means a buyer (other than for purposes of resale) of any consumer product, any person to whom such product is transferred during the duration of an implied or written warranty (or service contract) applicable to the product, and any other person who is entitled by the terms of such warranty (or service contract) or under applicable State law to enforce against the warrantor (or service contractor) the obligations of the warranty (or service contract). (4) The term "supplier" means any person engaged in the business of making a consumer product directly or indirectly available to consumers. (5) The term "warrantor" means any supplier or other person who gives or offers to give a written warranty or who is or may be obligated under an implied warranty. (6) The term "written warranty" means— (A) any written affirmation of fact or written promise made in connection with the sale of a consumer product by a supplier to a buyer which relates to the nature of the material or workmanship and affirms or promises that such material or workmanship is defect free or will meet a specified level of performance over a specified period of time, or (B) any undertaking in writing in connection with the sale by a supplier of a consumer product to refund, repair, replace, or take other remedial action with respect to such product in the event that such product fails to meet the specifications set forth in the undertaking, which written affirmati
perturbation.justification: The modified text attempts to enforce Delaware law regardless of conflict of law principles, potentially overriding consumer protection laws in jurisdictions with stronger ties. This contradicts the underlying principle of consumer protection embedded in laws like the Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act (15 USC Ch. 50), which aims to establish minimum standards across states. Although the snippet provided does not explicitly state the choice of law, it broadly covers 'CONSUMER PRODUCT WARRANTIES' and 'Federal minimum standards for warranties.' The modified contract language directly attempts to circumvent potentially more favorable consumer protection laws of other jurisdictions, creating a direct contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient agrees that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for an actual or threatened breach of this Agreement by the Recipient or its Representatives, and that any court having competent jurisdiction may enter a preliminary and/or permanent restraining order, injunction or order for specific performance, without posting any bond, in the event of an actual or threatened breach of any of the provisions of this Agreement, in addition to any other remedy at law or in equity available to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient agrees that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for an actual or threatened breach of this Agreement by the Recipient or its Representatives, and that any court having competent jurisdiction may enter a preliminary and/or permanent restraining order, injunction or order for specific performance, with posting a bond, in the event of an actual or threatened breach of any of the provisions of this Agreement, in addition to any other remedy at law or in equity available to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: By requiring the Disclosing Party to post a bond to obtain an injunction, it adds a hurdle that discourages the Disclosing Party from seeking injunctive relief, which reduces their legal recourse, thereby violating the duty of good faith and fair dealing implied in all contracts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Delaware law (Delaware Code Title 6 § 1-304). By requiring a bond for injunctive relief, the contract creates an unreasonable obstacle to the Disclosing Party's ability to enforce the agreement, thus violating the implied duty to act in good faith and deal fairly.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 6 > Article 1 Authenticated PDF General Provisions Part 1. General Provisions Part 2. General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation Part 3. Territorial Applicability and General Rules
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a requirement for the Disclosing Party to post a bond to obtain an injunction. This requirement adds a burden that discourages the Disclosing Party from seeking injunctive relief, potentially violating the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. The Law Explanation argues this violates Delaware Code Title 6 § 1-304. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing Delaware Code Online Title 6, supports the relevance of Delaware law. The Perturbation Explanation indicates this new burden reduces the legal recourse available to the Disclosing Party.

perturbation.original_text: All right, title and interest in and to a Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information will remain the exclusive property of the Disclosing Party and the Confidential Information will be held in trust and confidence by the Recipient and its Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: All right, title and interest in and to a Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information will remain the property of the Disclosing Party, and the Confidential Information will be held with reasonable care by the Recipient and its Representatives.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'exclusive property' to 'property' and 'trust and confidence' to 'reasonable care,' the clause weakens the protection of confidential information. This could violate trade secret laws, which require a higher standard of care to maintain confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: The change from 'exclusive property' and 'trust and confidence' to 'property' and 'reasonable care' weakens the trade secret protection. The Uniform Trade Secrets Act, as adopted in Delaware (Delaware Code Title 6, Chapter 20), requires a higher standard of care to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. 'Reasonable care' may not be sufficient to meet this standard, potentially leading to a violation of the Act if the information is misappropriated.

(2) âMisappropriationâ shall mean: a. Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or b. Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or 2. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade was: A. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; B. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or C. perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that the confidential information will be held in 'trust and confidence,' implying a high standard of care. The changed text weakens this standard by stating the information will be held with 'reasonable care.' The Law Explanation indicates that the Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires a higher standard of care to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets, which 'reasonable care' might not meet. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' and references 'a duty to maintain secrecy,' supporting the need for a strong confidentiality obligation. The change from 'trust and confidence' to 'reasonable care' potentially reduces the legal protection afforded to trade secrets, contradicting the requirement to maintain secrecy.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient will, within five business days of a written request by the Disclosing Party, confirm that it has destroyed the original provided to Recipient (and all copies made by it) any documents (in whatever
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient will, upon a written request by the Disclosing Party, use commercially reasonable efforts to destroy the original provided to Recipient (and all copies made by it) any documents (in whatever
perturbation.explanation: Changing the destruction confirmation from an obligation with a strict deadline ('within five business days') to a 'commercially reasonable efforts' standard weakens the obligation to return or destroy confidential information. This could violate intellectual property laws if the confidential information includes trade secrets, because it does not sufficiently ensure the confidentiality of those trade secrets. This weakened obligation could fail to prevent misappropriation.
perturbation.law_explanation: By weakening the destruction and return obligation to 'commercially reasonable efforts', the contract now potentially violates the Defend Trade Secrets Act (18 U.S. Code § 1836). This act requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, and a weaker obligation to destroy/return documents may not meet this standard, especially if commercially reasonable efforts are not diligently applied.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates a specific action (destruction of documents within five days), while the changed contract weakens this obligation to 'commercially reasonable efforts.' The Law Explanation states that this weakening potentially violates the Defend Trade Secrets Act, which requires 'reasonable measures' to protect trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 18 USC 1836, addresses civil proceedings for trade secret misappropriation. The change from a strict deadline to 'commercially reasonable efforts' could be interpreted as failing to provide reasonable measures to prevent misappropriation, therefore creating a contradiction with the intent, if not the precise letter, of the law.
